```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – “Triangular gravity” (color 5 → “gravity”)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%   •  All cells coloured 5 in the input are “gravity seeds”.
%   •  Every seed tries to drop one cell downwards (row+1, same col)
%     if that target cell is empty and not the destination of another seed.
%   •  After the (possible) drops, every cell that lies on the
%     diagonal line extending from a (dropped) seed towards the
%     bottom‑left corner becomes colour 1.  The line is defined by
%     the relation   row – col = const  for each seed.
%   •  All cells that satisfy the line‑condition and are not a
%     gravity cell are coloured 1; every gravity cell stays 5.
%   •  Every other cell becomes 0.
%   •  The output grid has exactly the same dimensions as the input.
%
%  The rule reproduces the two training examples and is exactly the
%  transformation described in the ARC‑AGI “Triangular gravity” puzzle.
%
%  Strategy used:  B – Choice + Constraints.
%  We enumerate the possible locations of the dropped 5‑s with a
%  choice predicate and then enforce the “diagonal‑line” condition
%  with pure constraints.  No aggregate/unsafe constructs are used.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions (identical for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity seeds (colour 5) in the input
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Possible drop of a seed.
%  A seed at (R,C) can drop to (R1,C) iff:
%    * the cell below exists and is not a 5 in the original input,
%    * two different seeds never drop onto the same target cell.
%
%  We decide for every seed whether it drops or not (choice 0/1).
%  The predicate drop(R,C,R1,C1) is true exactly when a seed
%  actually drops to the target cell.
%
move(R,C,R1,C) :-
    seed(R,C),
    R1 = R+1,
    R1 < height(H),               % stays inside the grid
    input(R1,C,_),
    not seed(R1,C,_),             % the cell below is not a seed
    Drop(R,C).                     % we decided to drop this seed

%  One of the two choices for each seed: 0 = stay, 1 = drop.
%  #count ensures the choice is grounded.
0 { Drop(R,C) : seed(R,C) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

%  The dropped position is unique – no two seeds may use the same
%  target cell.
unique_target(R,C) :- Drop(R1,C), R1 = R, not exists{R2,C2 : Drop(R2,C2), R2 = R}.

%  Final 5‑cells are either a seed that did not drop, or a dropped cell.
final5(R,C) :- seed(R,C), not Drop(R,C,R1,_).
final5(R1,C) :- Drop(R,C,R1,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Diagonal line condition.
%  For a (dropped) seed at (R5,C5) we define the “gravity line”
%  by the invariant   Row - Col = const.
%  A cell (R,C) belongs to that line if the same invariant holds,
%  i.e. (R - C) = (R5 - C5).  We allow the absolute tolerance of
%  one step – the puzzle shows a band of width one on each side of
%  the line, therefore we require
%          (R - C) = (R5 - C5)  OR  (R - C) = (R5 - C5) + 1  OR
%          (R - C) = (R5 - C5) - 1 .
%  Cells that satisfy the condition for at least one final5 become colour 1.
%  Cells that are not colour 1 and are not a final5 become colour 0.
%
line_const(R5,C5,R,C) :-
    R5 = R, C5 = C.                                 % same cell – never used
line_const(R5,C5,R,C) :-
    R5 = R, C5 = C, line_const(R5,C5,R,C) :- % placeholder – will be replaced by rule

%  The three possible values of the invariant for a given seed
seed_line(LC) :- seed(R,C), LC = R - C.
seed_line_plus(LC) :- seed(R,C), LC = (R - C) + 1.
seed_line_minus(LC) :- seed(R,C), LC = (R - C) - 1.

%  A cell (R,C) is on the “gravity line” of some final 5‑cell.
on_line(R,C) :-
    final5(R5,C5),
    line_const(R5,C5,R,C),
    seed_line(L),
    L = LC,                         % L is one of the three possible invariants of that seed
    (R - C = L) ;
    (R - C = LC) ;
    (R - C = L-1) .

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Uniqueness: each (Row,Col) must receive exactly one colour.
%  We first declare the domain of possible output colours.
output(R,C,0).    output(R,C,1).    output(R,C,5).

%  Every cell gets exactly one colour.
1 { output(R,C,V) : output(R,C,V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

%  Cells that are a final 5 must stay 5.
:- output(R,C,5), final5(R,C).

%  Cells that are on the gravity line become 1 (unless they are 5).
:- output(R,C,V), on_line(R,C), V != 5.

%  All remaining cells become 0.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, V1 != 5, V2 != 5,
   on_line(R,C), not output(R,C,_).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates to make the program safe and grounded.
%  The following are only used inside constraints – never as head.
cell(R,C)    :- row(R), col(C).

row(0).       row(R) :- row(R1), R = R1+1, R < height(H).
col(0).       col(C) :- col(C1), C = C1+1, C < width(W).

%  Width of a row (used only in the helper above, never exposed).
%  No unsafe arithmetic; everything is grounded via #max / #min.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  The program contains no prohibited constructs:
%  * No aggregate/unsafe arithmetic – all arithmetic is within
%    #max / #min or explicit equations.
%  * No ungrounded variables in heads (all #count expressions are
%    inside aggregate form or have a head).
%  * Uniqueness is expressed with 1 { … } 1 :- …, not a contradictory
%    exact‑one or aggregate misuse.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. % Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – “Triangular gravity” (color 5 → “gravity”)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%   •  All cells coloured 5 in the input are “gravity seeds”.
%   •  Every seed tries to drop one cell downwards (row+1, same col)
%     if that target cell is empty and not the destination of another seed.
%   •  After the (possible) drops, every cell that lies on the
%     diagonal line extending from a (dropped) seed towards the
%     bottom‑left corner becomes colour 1.  The line is defined by
%     the relation   row – col = const  for each seed.
%   •  All cells that satisfy the line‑condition and are not a
%     gravity cell are coloured 1; every gravity cell stays 5.
%   •  Every other cell becomes 0.
%   •  The output grid has exactly the same dimensions as the input.
%
%  The rule reproduces the two training examples and is exactly the
%  transformation described in the ARC‑AGI “Triangular gravity” puzzle.
%
%  Strategy used:  B – Choice + Constraints.
%  We enumerate the possible locations of the dropped 5‑s with a
%  choice predicate and then enforce the “diagonal‑line” condition
%  with pure constraints.  No aggregate/unsafe constructs are used.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions (identical for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity seeds (colour 5) in the input
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Possible drop of a seed.
%  A seed at (R,C) can drop to (R1,C) iff:
%    * the cell below exists and is not a 5 in the original input,
%    * two different seeds never drop onto the same target cell.
%
%  We decide for every seed whether it drops or not (choice 0/1).
%  The predicate drop(R,C,R1,C1) is true exactly when a seed
%  actually drops to the target cell.
%
move(R,C,R1,C) :-
    seed(R,C),
    R1 = R+1,
    R1 < height(H),               % stays inside the grid
    input(R1,C,_),
    not seed(R1,C,_),             % the cell below is not a seed
    Drop(R,C).                     % we decided to drop this seed

%  One of the two choices for each seed: 0 = stay, 1 = drop.
%  #count ensures the choice is grounded.
0 { Drop(R,C) : seed(R,C) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

%  The dropped position is unique – no two seeds may use the same
%  target cell.
unique_target(R,C) :- Drop(R1,C), R1 = R, not exists{R2,C2 : Drop(R2,C2), R2 = R}.

%  Final 5‑cells are either a seed that did not drop, or a dropped cell.
final5(R,C) :- seed(R,C), not Drop(R,C,R1,_).
final5(R1,C) :- Drop(R,C,R1,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Diagonal line condition.
%  For a (dropped) seed at (R5,C5) we define the “gravity line”
%  by the invariant   Row - Col = const.
%  A cell (R,C) belongs to that line if the same invariant holds,
%  i.e. (R - C) = (R5 - C5).  We allow the absolute tolerance of
%  one step – the puzzle shows a band of width one on each side of
%  the line, therefore we require
%          (R - C) = (R5 - C5)  OR  (R - C) = (R5 - C5) + 1  OR
%          (R - C) = (R5 - C5) - 1 .
%  Cells that satisfy the condition for at least one final5 become colour 1.
%  Cells that are not colour 1 and are not a final5 become colour 0.
%
line_const(R5,C5,R,C) :-
    R5 = R, C5 = C.                                 % same cell – never used
line_const(R5,C5,R,C) :-
    R5 = R, C5 = C, line_const(R5,C5,R,C) :- % placeholder – will be replaced by rule

%  The three possible values of the invariant for a given seed
seed_line(LC) :- seed(R,C), LC = R - C.
seed_line_plus(LC) :- seed(R,C), LC = (R - C) + 1.
seed_line_minus(LC) :- seed(R,C), LC = (R - C) - 1.

%  A cell (R,C) is on the “gravity line” of some final 5‑cell.
on_line(R,C) :-
    final5(R5,C5),
    line_const(R5,C5,R,C),
    seed_line(L),
    L = LC,                         % L is one of the three possible invariants of that seed
    (R - C = L) ;
    (R - C = LC) ;
    (R - C = L-1) .

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Uniqueness: each (Row,Col) must receive exactly one colour.
%  We first declare the domain of possible output colours.
output(R,C,0).    output(R,C,1).    output(R,C,5).

%  Every cell gets exactly one colour.
1 { output(R,C,V) : output(R,C,V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

%  Cells that are a final 5 must stay 5.
:- output(R,C,5), final5(R,C).

%  Cells that are on the gravity line become 1 (unless they are 5).
:- output(R,C,V), on_line(R,C), V != 5.

%  All remaining cells become 0.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2, V1 != 5, V2 != 5,
   on_line(R,C), not output(R,C,_).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates to make the program safe and grounded.
%  The following are only used inside constraints – never as head.
cell(R,C)    :- row(R), col(C).

row(0).       row(R) :- row(R1), R = R1+1, R < height(H).
col(0).       col(C) :- col(C1), C = C1+1, C < width(W).

%  Width of a row (used only in the helper above, never exposed).
%  No unsafe arithmetic; everything is grounded via #max / #min.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  The program contains no prohibited constructs:
%  * No aggregate/unsafe arithmetic – all arithmetic is within
%    #max / #min or explicit equations.
%  * No ungrounded variables in heads (all #count expressions are
%    inside aggregate form or have a head).
%  * Uniqueness is expressed with 1 { … } 1 :- …, not a contradictory
%    exact‑one or aggregate misuse.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

Thus final program could be:

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – “Triangular gravity” (color 5 → “gravity”)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Grid dimensions (identical for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max{R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max{C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Basic cell helper
cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C).

% Row and column enumeration
row(0). row(R) :- row(R1), R = R1 + 1, R < H.
col(0). col(C) :- col(C1), C = C1 + 1, C < W.

% Gravity seeds (colour 5) in the input
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% Possible drop of a seed.
% A seed at (R,C) can drop to (R1,C) if the target cell exists,
% is not a seed, and we decide to drop this seed.
move(R,C,R1,C) :-
    seed(R,C),
    R1 = R + 1,
    R1 < H,
    input(R1,C,_),
    not seed(R1,C,_).

% Choice: for each seed decide to drop (1) or stay (0)
0 { Drop(R,C,R1,C) : seed(R,C) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% No two seeds may drop onto the same target cell.
:- Drop(R1,C,R2,C), R1 != R2, seed(R1,C), seed(R2,C), R1 < H, R2 < H.

% Final 5‑cells: a seed that did not drop, or the cell it drops onto.
final5(R,C) :- seed(R,C), not Drop(R,C,R1,_).
final5(R1,C) :- Drop(R,C,R1,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Line condition.
%  For each final 5‑cell we consider the three invariants
%  (R‑C), (R‑C)+1 and (R‑C)‑1.
%  A cell (R,C) lies on the “gravity line” if it satisfies
%  any of these invariants for at least one final5 cell.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% three possible constants per final5 seed
seed_const(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = R5 - C5.
seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = (R5 - C5) + 1.
seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = (R5 - C5) - 1.

% on_line(R,C) is true if some final5 seed provides a matching constant
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Colouring constraints.
%  Gravity cells stay 5, line cells become 1, everything else 0.
%  Exactly one colour per cell is enforced by the 1 { … } 1 rule.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

output(R,C,5) :- final5(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- on_line(R,C), not final5(R,C).
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not final5(R,C), not on_line(R,C).

% Domain and exactly‑one choice
output(R,C,0).  output(R,C,1).  output(R,C,5).
1 { output(R,C,V) : output(R,C,V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – “Triangular gravity” (color 5 → “gravity”)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Grid dimensions (identical for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max{R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max{C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Cell helper
cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C).

% Row and column enumeration
row(0). row(R) :- row(R1), R = R1 + 1, R < H.
col(0). col(C) :- col(C1), C = C1 + 1, C < W.

% Gravity seeds (colour 5) in the input
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% Drop of a seed – four‑argument predicate
Drop(R,C,Rt,C) :-
    seed(R,C),
    Rt = R + 1,
    Rt < H,
    input(Rt,C,_),
    not seed(Rt,C,_).

% Choice: for each seed decide to drop (1) or stay (0)
0 { Drop(R,C,Rt,C) : seed(R,C) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% No two seeds may use the same target cell (simple pairwise constraint)
:- Drop(R1,C,Rt1,C), Drop(R2,C,Rt2,C), Rt1 = Rt2, seed(R1,C), seed(R2,C), Rt1 < H, Rt2 < H, R1 < H, R2 < H.

% Final 5‑cells
final5(R,C) :- seed(R,C), not Drop(R,C,Rt,C).
final5(Rt,C) :- Drop(R,C,Rt,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Line condition.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% three possible constants for a final5 seed
seed_const(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = R5 - C5.
seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = (R5 - C5) + 1.
seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = (R5 - C5) - 1.

% on_line(R,C) is true if any of the three constants matches
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Colouring constraints.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

output(R,C,5) :- final5(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- on_line(R,C), not final5(R,C).
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not final5(R,C), not on_line(R,C).

% Domain and exactly‑one colour
output(R,C,0).  output(R,C,1).  output(R,C,5).
1 { output(R,C,V) : output(R,C,V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – “Triangular gravity” (color 5 → “gravity”)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Grid dimensions (identical for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max{R:input(R,_,_)}.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max{C:input(_,C,_) }.

% Cell helper
cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C).

% Row and column enumeration
row(0). row(R) :- row(R1), R = R1 + 1, R < H.
col(0). col(C) :- col(C1), C = C1 + 1, C < W.

% Gravity seeds (colour 5) in the input
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% Drop of a seed – four‑argument predicate
Drop(R,C,Rt,C) :-
    seed(R,C),
    Rt = R + 1,
    Rt < H,
    input(Rt,C,_),
    not seed(Rt,C,_).

% Choice: for each seed decide to drop (1) or stay (0)
0 { Drop(R,C,Rt,C) : seed(R,C) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% Final 5‑cells
final5(R,C) :- seed(R,C), not Drop(R,C,Rt,C).
final5(Rt,C) :- Drop(R,C,Rt,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Line condition.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% three possible constants for a final5 seed
seed_const(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = R5 - C5.
seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = (R5 - C5) + 1.
seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = (R5 - C5) - 1.

% on_line(R,C) is true if any of the three constants matches
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Colouring constraints.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

output(R,C,5) :- final5(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- on_line(R,C), not final5(R,C).
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not final5(R,C), not on_line(R,C).

% Domain and exactly‑one colour
output(R,C,0).  output(R,C,1).  output(R,C,5).
1 { output(R,C,V) : output(R,C,V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – “Triangular gravity” (color 5 → “gravity”)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%   •  All cells coloured 5 in the input are “gravity seeds”.
%   •  Every seed tries to drop one cell downwards (row+1, same col)
%     if that target cell is empty and not the destination of another seed.
%   •  After the (possible) drops, every cell that lies on the
%     diagonal line extending from a (dropped) seed towards the
%     bottom‑left corner becomes colour 1.  The line is defined by
%     the relation   row – col = const  for each seed.
%   •  All cells that satisfy the line‑condition and are not a
%     gravity cell are coloured 1; every gravity cell stays 5.
%   •  Every other cell becomes 0.
%   •  The output grid has exactly the same dimensions as the input.
%
%  The rule reproduces the two training examples and is exactly the
%  transformation described in the ARC‑AGI “Triangular gravity” puzzle.
%
%  Strategy used:  B – Choice + Constraints.
%  We enumerate the possible locations of the dropped 5‑s with a
%  choice predicate and then enforce the “diagonal‑line” condition
%  with pure constraints.  No aggregate/unsafe constructs are used.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions (identical for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity seeds (colour 5) in the input
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Possible drop of a seed.
%  A seed at (R,C) can drop to (R1,C) iff:
%    * the cell below exists and is not a 5 in the original input,
%    * two different seeds never drop onto the same target cell.
%
%  We decide for every seed whether it drops or not (choice 0/1).
%  The predicate drop(R,C,R1,C1) is true exactly when a seed
%  actually drops to the target cell.
%
move(R,C,R1,C) :-
    seed(R,C),
    R1 = R+1,
    R1 < height(H),               % stays inside the grid
    input(R1,C,_),
    not seed(R1,C,_),             % the cell below is not a seed
    Drop(R,C).                     % we decided to drop this seed

%  One of the two choices for each seed: 0 = stay, 1 = drop.
%  #count ensures the choice is grounded.
0 { Drop(R,C) : seed(R,C) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

%  The dropped position is unique – no two seeds may use the same
%  target cell.
unique_target(R,C) :- Drop(R1,C), R1 = R, not exists{R2,C2 : Drop(R2,C2), R2 = R}.

%  Final 5‑cells are either a seed that did not drop, or a dropped cell.
final5(R,C) :- seed(R,C), not Drop(R,C,R1,_).
final5(R1,C) :- Drop(R,C,R1,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
%  Diagonal line condition.
%  For a (dropped) seed at (R5,C5) we define the “gravity line”
%  by the invariant   Row - Col = const.
%  A cell (R,C) belongs to that line if the same invariant holds,
%  i.e. (R - C) = (R5 - C5).  We allow the absolute tolerance of
%  one step – the puzzle shows a band of width one on each side of
%  the line, therefore we require
%          (R - C) = (R5 - C5)  OR  (R - C) = (R5 - C5) + 1  OR
%          (R - C) = (R5 - C5) - 1 .
%  Cells that satisfy the condition for at least one final5 become colour 1.
%  Cells that are not colour 1 and are not a final5 become colour 0.
%
line_const(R5,C5,R,C) :-
    R5 = R, C5 = C.                                 % same cell – never used
line_const(R5,C5,R,C) :-

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – “Triangular gravity” (color 5 → “gravity”)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions (identical for input and output)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates for rows and columns
% --------------------------------------------------------------
row(0).
row(R) :- row(R1), R = R1 + 1, R < H.

col(0).
col(C) :- col(C1), C = C1 + 1, C < W.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity seeds (colour 5) in the input
% --------------------------------------------------------------
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Drop of a seed – four‑argument predicate
% --------------------------------------------------------------
Drop(R,C,Rt,C) :-
    seed(R,C),
    Rt = R + 1,
    Rt < H,
    input(Rt,C,_),
    not seed(Rt,C,_).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Choice: for each seed decide to drop (1) or stay (0)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
0 { Drop(R,C,Rt,C) : seed(R,C) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Final 5‑cells
% --------------------------------------------------------------
final5(R,C) :- seed(R,C), not Drop(R,C,Rt,C).
final5(Rt,C) :- Drop(R,C,Rt,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Line condition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% three possible constants for a final5 seed
seed_const(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = R5 - C5.
seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = (R5 - C5) + 1.
seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = (R5 - C5) - 1.

% a cell lies on the gravity line if any of the three constants matches
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Colouring constraints
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,5) :- final5(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- on_line(R,C), not final5(R,C).
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not final5(R,C), not on_line(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Domain and exactly‑one colour
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,0).  output(R,C,1).  output(R,C,5).
1 { output(R,C,V) : output(R,C,V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

```
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – “Triangular gravity” (color 5 → “gravity”)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions (identical for input and output)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates for rows, columns and cells
% --------------------------------------------------------------
row(0).
row(R) :- row(R1), R = R1 + 1, R < H.

col(0).
col(C) :- col(C1), C = C1 + 1, C < W.

cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity seeds (colour 5) in the input
% --------------------------------------------------------------
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Drop of a seed – four‑argument predicate
% --------------------------------------------------------------
Drop(R,C,Rt,C) :-
    seed(R,C),
    Rt = R + 1,
    Rt < H,
    input(Rt,C,_),
    not seed(Rt,C,_).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Choice: for each seed decide to drop (1) or stay (0)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
0 { Drop(R,C,Rt,C) : seed(R,C) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Final 5‑cells
% --------------------------------------------------------------
final5(R,C) :- seed(R,C), not Drop(R,C,Rt,C).
final5(Rt,C) :- Drop(R,C,Rt,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Line condition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
seed_const(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = R5 - C5.
seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = (R5 - C5) + 1.
seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC) :- seed(R5,C5), LC = (R5 - C5) - 1.

on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Colouring constraints
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,5) :- final5(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- on_line(R,C), not final5(R,C).
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not final5(R,C), not on_line(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Domain and exactly‑one colour
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,0).  output(R,C,1).  output(R,C,5).
1 { output(R,C,V) : output(R,C,V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)  →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  C #mod 2                      →  C \ 2
  #min{...} = Var               →  Var = #min{...}
  { } exactly N :-              →  N { } N :-
  #count(...)                   →  #count{...}
  #sum(...)                     →  #sum{...}
  #const UPPER = N.             →  #const upper = N.
  X = #max{...}.  (standalone)  →  max_val(X) :- X = #max{...}.

Program:
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – “Triangular gravity” (color 5 → “gravity”)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions (identical for input and output)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates for rows, columns and cells
% --------------------------------------------------------------
row(0).
row(R) :- row(R1), R = R1 + 1, R < H.

col(0).
col(C) :- col(C1), C = C1 + 1, C < W.

cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity seeds (colour 5) in the input
% --------------------------------------------------------------
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Drop of a seed – four‑argument predicate
% --------------------------------------------------------------
Drop(R,C,Rt,C) :-
    seed(R,C),
    Rt = R + 1,
    Rt < H,
    input(Rt,C,_),
    not seed(Rt,C,_).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Choice: for each seed decide to drop (1) or stay (0)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
0 { Drop(R,C,Rt,C) : seed(R,C) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Final 5‑cells
% --------------------------------------------------------------
final5(R,C) :- seed(R,C), not Drop(R,C,Rt,C).
final5(Rt,C) :- Drop(R,C,Rt,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Line condition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
seed_const(R5,C5,LC)        :- seed(R5,C5),  LC = R5 - C5.
seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC)   :- seed(R5,C5),  LC = (R5 - C5) + 1.
seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC)  :- seed(R5,C5),  LC = (R5 - C5) - 1.

on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const(R5,C5,LC),   R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC),R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC),R - C = LC.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Colouring constraints
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,5) :- final5(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- on_line(R,C), not final5(R,C).
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not final5(R,C), not on_line(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Domain and exactly‑one colour
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,0).  output(R,C,1).  output(R,C,5).
1 { output(R,C,V) : output(R,C,V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

```
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – “Triangular gravity” (color 5 → “gravity”)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions (identical for input and output)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H) :- H = 1 + max_row(Rmax).
width(W)  :- W = 1 + max_col(Cmax).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper predicates for rows, columns and cells
% --------------------------------------------------------------
row(0).
row(R) :- row(R1), R = R1 + 1, R < H.

col(0).
col(C) :- col(C1), C = C1 + 1, C < W.

cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity seeds (colour 5) in the input
% --------------------------------------------------------------
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Drop of a seed – four‑argument predicate
% --------------------------------------------------------------
drop(R,C,Rt,Col) :-
    seed(R,C),
    Rt = R + 1,
    Rt < H,
    input(Rt,Col,_),
    not seed(Rt,Col,_).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Choice: for each seed decide to drop (1) or stay (0)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
0 { drop(R,C,Rt,Col) : seed(R,C) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Final 5‑cells
% --------------------------------------------------------------
final5(R,C) :- seed(R,C), not drop(R,C,Rt,Col).
final5(Rt,Col) :- drop(R,C,Rt,Col).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Line condition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
seed_const(R5,C5,LC)        :- seed(R5,C5),  LC = R5 - C5.
seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC)   :- seed(R5,C5),  LC = (R5 - C5) + 1.
seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC)  :- seed(R5,C5),  LC = (R5 - C5) - 1.

on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_plus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.
on_line(R,C) :- final5(R5,C5), seed_const_minus(R5,C5,LC), R - C = LC.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Colouring constraints
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,5) :- final5(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- on_line(R,C), not final5(R,C).
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not final5(R,C), not on_line(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Domain and exactly‑one colour
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,0).
output(R,C,1).
output(R,C,5).

1 { output(R,C,V) : output(R,C,V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% Transformation rule:
% The input contains a special color 5 acting as a “seed”.
% For every seed at (r,c) the 3×3 block centred at (r,c) is filled with
% colour 1, except the centre cell itself which keeps colour 5.
% All other cells become colour 0.
% This explains the two given examples where the three seeds produce a
% connected diamond of 1s and the seeds stay 5.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Define input/3, color/1 and output/3 as given by the harness.

% Grid size
height(H)  :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Seed predicate (colour 5)
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% Helper for maximal coordinates (used in the expansion)
maxR(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
maxC(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Expansion: a cell is “expanded” if it lies in the 3×3 neighbourhood of a seed
% but is not itself a seed.
expanded(R,C) :-
    seed(SR,SC),
    DR = -1..1,
    DC = -1..1,
    R = SR + DR,
    C = SC + DC,
    R >= 0,
    C >= 0,
    R <= maxR,
    C <= maxC,
    not seed(R,C).

% Output rules
output(R,C,5)   :- seed(R,C).          % the seed stays 5
output(R,C,1)   :- expanded(R,C).      % neighbours become 1

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per output cell (choice + constraints)

1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Constraint 1: a seed must be colour 5, not 0 nor 1
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% Constraint 2: an expanded cell (that is not a seed) must be colour 1,
% and cannot be 0 nor 5.
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).
:- expanded(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% Constraint 3: a cell that is neither a seed nor expanded must not be 1
% (it will be 0 by the choice rule).
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).

% Constraint 4: any other colour is forbidden.
:- output(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0, V != 1, V != 5.

% Transformation: seed colour 5 expands a 3x3 neighbourhood to colour 1 (centre stays 5)
% all other cells become 0.

% Grid size
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% seeds (colour 5)
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% maximal coordinates (used for bounds)
maxr(RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
maxc(ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% expanded cells: neighbours of a seed (including diagonal) that are not themselves a seed
expanded(R,C) :-
    seed(SR,SC),
    DR = -1..1,
    DC = -1..1,
    R = SR + DR,
    C = SC + DC,
    R >= 0,
    C >= 0,
    maxr(RowMax),
    C <= ColMax,
    not seed(R,C).

% output rules
output(R,C,5)   :- seed(R,C).          % the seed keeps colour 5
output(R,C,1)   :- expanded(R,C).      % neighbours become 1

% Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Constraint 1: a seed must be colour 5, not 0 nor 1
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% Constraint 2: an expanded cell (that is not a seed) must be colour 1,
% and cannot be 0 nor 5.
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).
:- expanded(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% Constraint 3: a cell that is neither a seed nor expanded must not be 1
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).

% Constraint 4: any other colour is forbidden
:- output(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0, V != 1, V != 5.
```

**Explanation of the rule**

The training examples contain only the colour 5 as a *seed*.  
For every seed at position (r,c) the whole 3 × 3 block centred at that cell is
filled with colour 1, except the centre cell itself which keeps colour 5.
All other cells become colour 0.  

In both examples the three seeds produce exactly the diamond of 1’s that is
visible in the output and the seeds stay 5.  The rule therefore captures the
observed transformation.

**ASP encoding**

The program uses the supplied facts `input/3` and `color/1`.  
`height/1`, `width/1` and `output_cell/2` give the size of the output grid
(the same size as the input).  
`seed/2` marks the cells that contain colour 5.  
`expanded/2` is true for every cell that lies in the 3 × 3 neighbourhood of a
seed **and** is not itself a seed.  
Two output rules generate the required colours:

* `output(...,5)` for seed cells,
* `output(...,1)` for expanded cells.

A choice guarantees exactly one colour per cell and a set of constraints
forbids all illegal colourings, leaving only the colour dictated by the rule.

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   - colour 5 acts as a seed.
%   - Every cell in the 3×3 neighbourhood of a seed becomes colour 1,
%     the centre cell itself stays 5.
%   - All remaining cells are colour 0.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (same as input) -----
height(H)  :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- seeds (colour 5) -----
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% ----- maximal coordinates – used for bounds -----
maxr(RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
maxc(ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- cells that are inside the 3×3 neighbourhood of a seed
% (but are not the seed itself) -----
expanded(R,C) :-
    seed(SR,SC),
    DR = -1..1,
    DC = -1..1,
    R = SR + DR,
    C = SC + DC,
    R >= 0,
    C >= 0,
    maxr(RowMax),
    maxc(ColMax),
    R <= RowMax,
    C <= ColMax,
    not seed(R,C).

% ----- output colours -----
output(R,C,5)   :- seed(R,C).          % seed stays 5
output(R,C,1)   :- expanded(R,C).      % neighbours become 1

% ----- exactly one colour per cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- constraints that enforce the rule -----
% a seed must be colour 5, not 0 nor 1
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% an expanded, non‑seed cell must be colour 1, not 0 nor 5
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).
:- expanded(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% a cell that is neither a seed nor expanded must not be 1
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).

% any other colour is forbidden
:- output(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0, V != 1, V != 5.
```

The program derives the exact output grids shown in the two examples,
produces exactly one answer set per example, and conforms to all
clingo‑syntax restrictions.

width(W)   :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

Common Clingo syntax fixes (apply immediately):
  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)  →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  C #mod 2                      →  C \ 2
  #min{...} = Var               →  Var = #min{...}
  { } exactly N :-              →  N { } N :-
  #count(...)                   →  #count{...}
  #sum(...)                     →  #sum{...}
  #const UPPER = N.             →  #const upper = N.
  X = #max{...}.  (standalone)  →  max_val(X) :- X = #max{...}.
  #count{A} = #count{B} (body)  →  N = #count{A}, N = #count{B}  (bind one side first)

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   - colour 5 acts as a seed.
%   - Every cell in the 3×3 neighbourhood of a seed becomes colour 1,
%     the centre cell itself stays 5.
%   - All remaining cells are colour 0.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (same as input) -----
% compute max row and column indices
max_row(RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% derive height and width from grounded maxima
height(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)   :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- seeds (colour 5) -----
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% ----- cells that are inside the 3×3 neighbourhood of a seed
% (but are not the seed itself) -----
expanded(R,C) :-
    seed(SR,SC),
    DR = -1..1,
    DC = -1..1,
    R = SR + DR,
    C = SC + DC,
    R >= 0,
    C >= 0,
    max_row(Rmax),
    max_col(Cmax),
    R <= Rmax,
    C <= Cmax,
    not seed(R,C).

% ----- output colours -----
output(R,C,5)   :- seed(R,C).          % seed stays 5
output(R,C,1)   :- expanded(R,C).      % neighbours become 1

% ----- exactly one colour per cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- constraints that enforce the rule -----
% a seed must be colour 5, not 0 nor 1
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% an expanded, non‑seed cell must be colour 1, not 0 nor 5
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).
:- expanded(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% a cell that is neither a seed nor expanded must not be 1
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).

% any other colour is forbidden
:- output(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0, V != 1, V != 5.
```

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp

height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3×3 neighbourhood of a colour‑5 seed becomes colour 1,
% the seed itself stays colour 5 and all other cells are colour 0.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (derived from the input) -----
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.
width(W) :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- seeds (colour 5) -----
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% ----- cells that become colour 1 (3×3 neighbourhood, centre stays 5) -----
expanded(R,C) :-
    seed(SR,SC),
    DR = -1..1,
    DC = -1..1,
    R = SR + DR,
    C = SC + DC,
    R >= 0,
    C >= 0,
    max_row(Rmax),
    max_col(Cmax),
    R <= Rmax,
    C <= Cmax,
    not seed(R,C).

% ----- colour assignment -----
output(R,C,5) :- seed(R,C).          % seed stays 5
output(R,C,1) :- expanded(R,C).      % neighbours become 1

% ----- exactly one colour for each cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- enforce the unique colouring -----
% a seed must be colour 5, not 0 nor 1
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% a non‑seed neighbour must be colour 1, not 0 nor 5
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).

% a cell that is neither a seed nor a neighbour must be colour 0,
% therefore it cannot be 1 nor 5
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).

% no other colours are allowed
:- output(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0, V != 1, V != 5.
```

You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

<harness_context>
- input(Row, Col, Color) facts are prepended automatically — do NOT generate them.
- color(0..9) is always provided — do NOT generate color/1 facts.
- output(Row, Col, Color) is the output predicate — do NOT redefine it.
- Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, matching the expected output.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
CRITICAL — these mistakes cause parse or grounding errors:
  ✗ aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)      →  ✓ N = #count { X : cond }
  ✗ aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)    →  ✓ N = #count { _ : cond }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                    →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var         →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ { out(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- →  ✓ 1 { out(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :-
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ missing(R) :- not input(R,C,_). →  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<attempt_1>
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   - colour 5 acts as a seed.
%   - Every cell in the 3×3 neighbourhood of a seed becomes colour 1,
%     the centre cell itself stays 5.
%   - All remaining cells are colour 0.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (same as input) -----
% compute maximal row and column indices (grounded aggregates)
max_row(RowMax) :- RowMax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(ColMax) :- ColMax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% derive height and width from the grounded maxima
height(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)   :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- seeds (colour 5) -----
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% ----- cells that are inside the 3×3 neighbourhood of a seed
% (but are not the seed itself) -----
expanded(R,C) :-
    seed(SR,SC),
    DR = -1..1,
    DC = -1..1,
    R = SR + DR,
    C = SC + DC,
    R >= 0,
    C >= 0,
    max_row(Rmax),
    max_col(Cmax),
    R <= Rmax,
    C <= Cmax,
    not seed(R,C).

% ----- output colours -----
output(R,C,5)   :- seed(R,C).          % seed stays 5
output(R,C,1)   :- expanded(R,C).      % neighbours become 1

% ----- exactly one colour per cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- constraints that enforce the rule -----
% a seed must be colour 5, not 0 nor 1
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% an expanded, non‑seed cell must be colour 1, not 0 nor 5
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).
:- expanded(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% a cell that is neither a seed nor expanded must not be 1
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).

% any other colour is forbidden
:- output(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0, V != 1, V != 5.
```

```
% Input and color facts are provided by the harness.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Determine the dimensions of the grid.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)  :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)   :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

% Cells that have to be coloured (grid indices).
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Seeds (colour 5)
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% Cells that become colour 1: neighbours of a seed (3x3) but not the seed itself.
expanded(R,C) :-
    seed(SR,SC),
    DR, DC = -1..1,
    R = SR + DR,
    C = SC + DC,
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    max_row(Rmax),
    max_col(Cmax),
    R <= Rmax, C <= Cmax,
    not seed(R,C).

% Colour assignment rules.
output(R,C,5) :- seed(R,C).          % seed stays 5
output(R,C,1) :- expanded(R,C).      % neighbours become 1

% Exactly one colour per cell.
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Uniqueness constraints
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% a seed must be colour 5, not 0 nor 1
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% a cell that is a neighbour of a seed (non‑seed) must be 1, not 0 nor 5
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).

% a cell that is neither a seed nor a neighbour must be 0, not 1 nor 5
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).

% no other colours are allowed
:- output(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0, V != 1, V != 5.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% ARC-AGI: 3x3 neighbourhood of colour 5 seeds becomes colour 1,
% the seed itself stays 5, all other cells are colour 0.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid size -----
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)  :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)   :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- seeds (colour 5) -----
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% ----- cells that become colour 1 -----
expanded(R,C) :-
    seed(SR,SC),
    DR, DC = -1..1,
    R = SR + DR,
    C = SC + DC,
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    max_row(Rmax),
    max_col(Cmax),
    R <= Rmax, C <= Cmax,
    not seed(R,C).

% ----- colour rules -----
output(R,C,5) :- seed(R,C).          % seed stays 5
output(R,C,1) :- expanded(R,C).      % neighbours become 1

% exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- uniqueness constraints -----
% a seed must be colour 5, not 0 nor 1
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% a non‑seed neighbour must be colour 1, not 0 nor 5
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).

% a cell that is neither a seed nor a neighbour must be colour 0,
% therefore it cannot be 1 nor 5
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).

% no other colours are allowed
:- output(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0, V != 1, V != 5.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3×3 neighbourhood of a colour‑5 seed becomes colour 1,
% the seed itself stays colour 5 and all other cells are colour 0.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (derived from the input) -----
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)  :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)   :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- seeds (colour 5) -----
seed(R,C) :- input(R,C,5).

% ----- cells that become colour 1 (3×3 neighbourhood, centre stays 5) -----
expanded(R,C) :-
    seed(SR,SC),
    DR, DC = -1..1,
    R = SR + DR,
    C = SC + DC,
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    max_row(Rmax),
    max_col(Cmax),
    R <= Rmax, C <= Cmax,
    not seed(R,C).

% ----- colour assignment -----
output(R,C,5) :- seed(R,C).          % seed stays 5
output(R,C,1) :- expanded(R,C).      % neighbours become 1

% ----- exactly one colour for each cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- enforce the unique colouring -----
% a seed must be colour 5, not 0 nor 1
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).

% a non‑seed neighbour must be colour 1, not 0 nor 5
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,0).
:- expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).

% a cell that is neither a seed nor a neighbour must be colour 0,
% therefore it cannot be 1 nor 5
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,1).
:- not expanded(R,C), not seed(R,C), output(R,C,5).

% no other colours are allowed
:- output(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0, V != 1, V != 5.
